one_line_takeaway: A document sitting in an LCLM's input context leaks its own membership: prompting the model with a short snippet makes generation loss and output/document similarity strongly separate members from non-members, yielding up to ~90.7% F1 even in text-only settings.
key_finding: Against the LongChat-7b-v1.5-32k victim on the Natural Question multi-document QA benchmark (with the MIA target document deliberately placed in the middle of the documents, the hardest 'lost-in-the-middle' position), the Meta-Classifier is the strongest attack, exceeding 90% accuracy across 10/20/30-document tasks and reaching a 90.66% F1-score (91.10% accuracy) on 30-document QA. The text-only Bert Score and Sacre BLEU attacks also achieve F1 > 85% across all three document counts despite not using logits, supporting the retrieval-activation hypothesis. In contrast, the Logits Attack and Inquiry Attack (which use the whole document and force a single yes/no token) perform much worse, with accuracy below 70%, which the authors attribute to under-using the model's retrieval capacity and to yes/no position bias.
core_contribution: The paper presents what it claims is the first membership inference attack (MIA) targeting the external input context of Long-Context Language Models (LCLMs), rather than their training data. The attacker's aim is to decide whether a given target document x_t is present in the model's prepended long context C. The authors propose six attack methods split into two access scenarios: (1) probability-based attacks that require generation logits/loss (Logits Attack, Loss Attack, and a Meta-Classifier), and (2) text-only attacks that use only the returned text (Inquiry Attack, Bert Score Attack, Sacre BLEU Attack). The central intuition is that if a document is in the context, prompting the model with a short snippet from it strongly activates the model's retrieval behavior, causing generated continuations to have low generation loss and high semantic similarity to the true document, whereas non-member documents do not.
threat_model: The attacker is given a target document x_t and a victim LCLM M, and must output Member/Non-member for whether x_t is in the input long context C. Attacker knowledge: NO access to model parameters, inference hidden states, or any information about the prepended long context. Attacker capacity: can query the victim LCLM and obtain output text; the probability-based variants additionally assume access to output token prediction probabilities (as in prior works [12,25]), while the text-only variants assume only returned text is available. Attacks are launched by injecting an 'attack prompt' after the user question inside the standard QA system prompt (e.g., asking the model to complete a prefix of x_t, or to answer yes/no about having seen x_t).
limitations: The detailed quantitative evidence in the paper is a single table (Table 1) for one victim model (LongChat-7b-v1.5-32k) on one benchmark family (NaturalQuestion multi-document QA); although the paper lists a broad model roster (LongChat-7b-v1.5-64k, LongChat-13b-16k, Vicuna-13b-v1.5-16k, Mistral-7b-Instruct-v0.2, Gemma-1.1-7b-it, Qwen1.5-7b-chat) and additional datasets (KV-retrieval, LongBench with 16 tasks), no per-model or per-dataset results for those appear in the fetched pages, so the breadth of the empirical claim is not fully substantiated by the shown tables. Non-members are drawn from a different dataset than members, which raises a distribution-shift confound (the attack may partly detect topic/domain difference rather than pure membership). The strongest attack (Meta-Classifier) requires both logit access and a labeled reference dataset, weakening the black-box realism claim. No defenses or mitigations are evaluated; the conclusion only gestures toward future robust defenses. This is a short (7-page) arXiv preprint (v1, Nov 2024) and contains several typos.

datasets_used: Natural Question multi-document QA (from 'Lost in the Middle' [7]) with 10/20/30 documents, gold/target document in the middle position
LongBench [29] (16 tasks: multi-doc QA, single-doc QA, summarization, few-shot learning, synthetic tasks, code completion; avg length ~37k tokens)
quantitative_results: Meta-Classifier (probability-based), LongChat-7b-v1.5-32k, 30-Document QA: F1 90.66%, Accuracy 91.10%, Precision 95.36%, Recall 86.40% (headline number)
Meta-Classifier, 10-Document QA: Accuracy 92.40%, F1 92.01%; 20-Document QA: Accuracy 91.90%, F1 91.84%
Logits Attack accuracy: 70.50% / 68.20% / 65.30% (10/20/30-doc) — high recall (~99%) but low precision (~59-63%)
Inquiry Attack accuracy: 69.20% / 66.90% / 63.30% (10/20/30-doc), the weakest method
Meta-Classifier feature vector = 15 values (loss + BERTScore + BLEU at document splits of 2/4/6/8/10 pieces); trained on 1,000 reference samples, tested on 1,000 samples
baselines_compared: The six proposed attacks are compared against each other (Logits, Loss, Meta-Classifier vs Inquiry, Bert Score, Sacre BLEU); the Meta-Classifier is the internal best baseline
Text-only attacks (Bert Score, Sacre BLEU) serve as the realistic-scenario comparison against probability-based attacks

No external prior-MIA method is reported as a numeric baseline in the extracted table; the paper positions itself against existing MIAs for classification/generative LLMs, RAG [23,24], and in-context learning [25], noting those rely on training-set overfitting which LCLM contexts lack
